data.title: brainstorm Phase 1 Gate: Understanding Summary carries unresolved scope ambiguity -> /dso:implementation-plan listed as in-scope with outcome TBD, violating the loop's purpose of reducing ambiguity before approaches
data.description:
## Summary

The /dso:brainstorm Phase 1 Gate Step 1 (Understanding Summary) is supposed to resolve ambiguity before moving to Phase 2 approaches. During a live session on 2026-04-17, the executor presented an Understanding Summary that explicitly carried forward a significant unresolved scope question — whether /dso:implementation-plan is in scope, and what changes (if any) are required there. The summary said the outcome could validly be no changes. That is not a resolved scope; it is a deferred question dressed up as a scope bullet.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Invoke /dso:brainstorm with free-text describing a planning-pipeline gap that spans multiple skills (brainstorm, preplanning, implementation-plan, sprint).
2. During the Phase 1 dialogue, when the user says check whether implementation-plan needs changes but does not make it a hard commitment, observe how the executor renders this in the Understanding Summary.

## Expected Behavior

Phase 1 Gate Step 1 should present a summary where every in-scope item has a resolved scope claim. If a scope decision cannot be made without investigation, the executor should EITHER (a) do the investigation during Phase 1 before presenting the summary, OR (b) ask one more Socratic question to resolve it, OR (c) explicitly move the item to a deferred/investigate-during-scrutiny list that is clearly separated from the in-scope list.

The loop's purpose is to drive ambiguity to zero before approaches are proposed. Carrying a verify-whether-changes-are-required bullet into the in-scope list leaks the ambiguity into Phase 2, where it gets baked into a proposed approach without ever being decided.

## Actual Behavior

The Understanding Summary listed /dso:implementation-plan under Scope - in with the text: verify whether any changes are required; valid outcome is no changes if current framing already handles these story shapes.

This is not a scope bullet. It is a research task whose outcome shapes scope. Treating its uncertainty as acceptable contradicts the gate's purpose.

## Rationalization the Executor Used (anti-pattern to catch)

The executor justified the bullet by citing the user's earlier instruction (It is a valid finding that no changes are needed to implementation-plan, but we should check now) and treated Ill check during scrutiny as sufficient. It is not — scrutiny (Phase 2 Step 2.5 onward) validates an already-scoped spec; it does not replace Phase 1 intent resolution. The executor conflated investigation-during-scrutiny with scope-is-resolved.

## Suspected Root Cause

Phase 1 Gate Step 1 in skills/brainstorm/SKILL.md does not explicitly prohibit unresolved-scope bullets. The instructions say the summary covers problem, users, scope, and success but do not gate on whether every scope entry is decided. Fix candidates:

- Add an explicit rule: Every item in Scope - in must name a concrete change or deliverable. Items whose scope depends on investigation MUST be moved to a separate Pending Investigation list until resolved, or the investigation must be performed before presenting the summary.
- Add an example of the anti-pattern (verify whether X needs changes) being called out.
- Add a self-check: Before rendering the summary, scan each scope bullet for verify/check/investigate/TBD keywords; if any appear, either investigate inline or ask a gap question.

## Impact

Leaks planning ambiguity into Phase 2; downstream approaches get proposed against an undecided scope; the spec reviewers (fidelity, feasibility, red/blue team) waste cycles on content that may or may not be in the final epic; practitioners get false confidence that Phase 1 resolved the intent.
